q: In scikit-learn, what is the critical rule for applying feature scaling to avoid data leakage?
options: Fit the scaler only on training data, then transform both train and test sets using those statistics
Fit the scaler on the full dataset before any split, inadvertently embedding test-set statistics into the scaler
Fit separate scalers on training and test sets independently to respect their distinct statistics
Apply the scaler transformation only to numeric columns and leave categorical columns untouched
explanation: Fitting on the full dataset leaks test-set statistics (mean, std) into training, inflating scores. The scaler must see only training data.

q: What output does StandardScaler produce after transformation?
options: Values bounded in the range [0, 1] using the feature's observed minimum and maximum
Features with zero mean and unit standard deviation (Z-score normalization)
Values ranked by percentile to produce a uniform distribution
Features log-transformed to remove right skew from the original distribution
explanation: StandardScaler computes (x − mean) / std per feature, yielding mean=0, std=1. MinMaxScaler, not StandardScaler, produces the [0,1] range.

q: Which classification metric is most critical when false negatives carry the highest cost, as in a cancer-screening model?
options: Precision, which penalizes each false positive prediction made by the classifier
F1-score, which is the harmonic mean of precision and recall combined
Recall, which measures what fraction of actual positives the model correctly identifies
Accuracy, which reflects overall correctness across all classes in the dataset
explanation: Recall = TP / (TP + FN). Maximizing recall minimizes missed positive cases (false negatives), which is critical in medical screening.

q: Data drift (covariate shift) in a deployed ML model means that:
options: Model weights slowly degrade in precision due to floating-point rounding over time
The relationship between input features and the target label changes after deployment
Training and serving pipelines apply inconsistent feature transformations to the data
The distribution of input features P(X) changes relative to what the model was trained on
explanation: Data drift = shift in P(X); concept drift = shift in P(Y|X). Both degrade performance but require different responses.

q: DBSCAN's primary advantage over K-Means when clustering crescent-shaped data is:
options: It automatically determines the number of clusters and marks isolated points as noise
It runs faster on large datasets because it only computes distances to cluster centroids
It guarantees a globally optimal partition by minimizing total within-cluster variance
It requires no hyperparameter tuning whatsoever and works identically on any feature space
explanation: DBSCAN discovers arbitrary-shaped clusters and labels outliers as -1 (noise) without needing a pre-specified K; K-Means would badly split crescent shapes.

q: What is the correct lifecycle order for a model in the MLflow Model Registry?
options: Development → Testing → Staging → Archived → Production
Training run → Registered → Staging → Production → Archived
Prototype → Registered → Validation → Canary → Production
Experiment → Baseline → Candidate → Champion → Retired
explanation: MLflow stages are: register after a training run, promote through Staging for review, then Production for serving, then Archived when superseded.

q: How is overfitting identified in a learning curve plot?
options: Both training and validation accuracy are low and plateau with increasing data
Both training and validation accuracy are high and converge as more data is added
Training accuracy is high but validation accuracy is much lower, showing a large gap
Validation accuracy consistently exceeds training accuracy at small dataset sizes
explanation: Overfitting: model memorizes training data (high train score) but generalizes poorly (low val score). Fix: regularize, add data, or simplify the model.

q: KNN is called a 'lazy learner' because:
options: It uses a random subset of features at prediction time to reduce compute overhead
It converges slowly during optimization because it iterates through many gradient descent weight-update steps across epochs
It builds a compressed decision boundary rather than memorizing all training points
It defers all computation to prediction time, storing training data and computing distances on the fly
explanation: KNN has no training phase; it stores all training samples and computes distances to each at prediction, making inference O(n) per query.

q: In the K-Means Elbow Method, what does the 'elbow' represent?
options: The K value beyond which additional clusters yield diminishing reductions in inertia
The K value where the silhouette score reaches its global maximum across tested values
The inflection point where training time increases sharply with each additional cluster
The point where cluster centroids stop updating during the iterative assignment step
explanation: Inertia always decreases with more clusters, but the elbow marks where the marginal gain drops sharply—a heuristic for the best K.

q: Which encoding strategy is best suited for a categorical feature with 500 unique cities?
options: Label Encoding, assigning integers 0–499 in alphabetical order to the city names
Target Encoding, replacing each city with the mean of the target variable for that city
One-Hot Encoding, which creates 500 binary columns for the city feature
Binary Encoding, which maps city names to binary codes using a hash function
explanation: One-Hot would add 500 sparse columns, causing dimensionality explosion. Target Encoding compresses to one column while retaining predictive signal.

q: Decision trees do NOT require feature scaling because:
options: They normalize inputs internally as part of their Gini/entropy impurity calculation
They use Euclidean distance internally and handle scale implicitly via depth limits
They split on feature value thresholds, so relative magnitudes between features are irrelevant
They are immune to multicollinearity, which is the primary reason scaling is needed
explanation: Tree splits are threshold comparisons (feature_x > 2.5), not distance calculations, so multiplying a feature by 1000 does not change any split.

q: Why does wrapping preprocessing inside a scikit-learn Pipeline prevent data leakage during cross-validation?
options: Pipelines cache preprocessed arrays so each fold avoids redundant feature transformations
Pipeline steps are stateless by design, so no information passes between cross-validation folds
Cross-validation with a pipeline automatically applies stratification to prevent leakage
Each fold's scaler/imputer is fit only on that fold's training portion, never seeing validation data
explanation: cross_val_score re-fits the entire pipeline per fold, so statistics (mean, std) are computed only from the training split of that fold.

q: A PSI value above 0.20 in production feature monitoring signals:
options: A major distribution shift requiring immediate investigation and likely model retraining
A minor distributional fluctuation within acceptable bounds for continued deployment
A moderate shift worth monitoring but not requiring any corrective action
Statistical noise that can be safely ignored during routine monitoring intervals
explanation: PSI thresholds: <0.10 = stable, 0.10–0.20 = moderate (investigate), >0.20 = major shift (retrain or flag for review).

q: How do Grid Search and Randomized Search fundamentally differ in hyperparameter tuning?
options: Grid Search evaluates each parameter independently; Random Search optimizes them jointly
Grid Search tries every combination in the grid; Randomized Search samples N random combinations
Grid Search supports only numeric parameters; Randomized Search handles categorical ranges too
Grid Search parallelizes across all CPU cores; Randomized Search is strictly sequential
explanation: Grid Search is exhaustive (e.g., 3×4×3×3 = 108 runs); Randomized Search evaluates a budget of N random configs, typically far fewer.

q: Applying PolynomialFeatures(degree=2, include_bias=False) to two features [age, fare] creates how many output features?
options: 2 features, since degree=2 retains only the squared versions of the originals
4 features: age, fare, age², fare² without any interaction term
5 features: age, fare, age², fare², and the interaction term age×fare
8 features: all degree-0, degree-1, and degree-2 monomials of the two inputs
explanation: degree=2 with 2 features and include_bias=False gives: age, fare, age², fare², age*fare = 5 features.

q: After a logistic regression baseline, which algorithm does this chapter recommend as the first 'serious' model for tabular data?
options: SVM with RBF kernel, for its strong theoretical margin-maximization guarantees
K-Nearest Neighbors (k=5) because it needs almost no hyperparameter tuning
A two-layer neural network that typically beats shallow methods on structured data
Random Forest, which gives high accuracy out of the box with minimal tuning
explanation: Random Forest trains 100+ trees in parallel, handles mixed feature types, resists overfitting, and needs little tuning—ideal after a baseline.

q: In the FastAPI serving endpoint, Pydantic's primary role is to:
options: Validate and parse incoming request data, rejecting malformed inputs before they reach the model
Serialize each model prediction to a well-formed JSON response body before writing it to the HTTP socket
Load the pipeline artifact from disk and cache it in application memory at startup
Route requests to different model versions based on the user's request headers
explanation: Pydantic validates types, ranges (ge/le), and patterns in the request body, raising HTTP 422 automatically for invalid inputs—protecting the model.

q: A silhouette score above which threshold indicates 'strong cluster structure'?
options: Above 0.25, indicating that some cluster structure exists in the data
Above 0.70, indicating compact, well-separated clusters
Above 0.50, indicating a reasonable but not excellent clustering solution
Above 0.90, which is the minimum for statistical significance of cluster separation
explanation: Silhouette scale: <0.25 = weak, 0.25–0.50 = reasonable, 0.50–0.70 = reasonable, >0.70 = strong. Values near 1 mean points are much closer to their own cluster.

q: A model achieves 99% training accuracy but only 60% test accuracy. What is the most appropriate first intervention?
options: Add more input features to increase representational capacity on the training set
Replace the model with a deeper neural network to capture more complex patterns
Reduce model complexity (e.g., limit tree depth, increase min_samples_leaf) or add regularization
Decrease the learning rate and train for additional epochs to improve weight convergence
explanation: A large train-test gap is the hallmark of overfitting. The fix is to constrain model capacity or add regularization, not to increase it.

q: In a canary deployment for ML, the production rollout strategy is to:
options: Run the new model in shadow mode alongside production, receiving all traffic without returning its responses to real users
Switch all production traffic instantly to the new model upon passing all automated tests
Deploy two identical model replicas and load-balance traffic equally between them
Route a small traffic slice (e.g., 5%) to the new model and gradually increase it while monitoring metrics
explanation: Canary grows v2 traffic from 5%→20%→50%→100%, catching regressions early. Shadow mode (option A) serves zero real users.

q: In KNN, why does prediction performance degrade as dataset size grows to millions of rows?
options: Each prediction scans all training points to compute distances, so latency scales as O(n) per query
The model must fully rebuild its internal distance index after every new data point is appended to the training set
The algorithm's O(n²) pairwise matrix must be recomputed at each prediction call
The kernel trick used internally requires O(n³) matrix inversion for new test predictions
explanation: KNN is a lazy learner with no training phase—all computation happens at inference. For n=1M training points, each prediction computes 1M distances.

q: LightGBM controls model complexity primarily through which parameter, unlike most gradient boosting libraries?
options: colsample_bytree, which randomly samples feature columns at each level of tree construction
num_leaves, which limits the maximum number of leaf nodes per tree rather than tree depth
subsample, which introduces stochastic row sampling noise into each tree's construction
min_child_samples, which is directly equivalent to max_depth in XGBoost's tree building
explanation: LightGBM grows trees leaf-wise (best-first), so num_leaves is the primary complexity parameter. XGBoost/Sklearn GB use max_depth (level-wise growth).

q: The scikit-learn ColumnTransformer is used to:
options: Chain multiple models sequentially, passing the output of each as input to the next
Apply a single transformation to one specified column while leaving all others untouched
Apply different preprocessing pipelines to different subsets of columns simultaneously
Cast column data types from strings to numeric values using built-in pandas parsing rules
explanation: ColumnTransformer dispatches numeric_pipeline to numeric columns and categorical_pipeline to categorical columns in one object, enabling mixed preprocessing.

q: The adjusted_rand_score is the appropriate clustering evaluation metric when:
options: No ground-truth labels exist and you need an internal cohesion measure across clusters
Comparing silhouette scores of K-Means and DBSCAN on the same unlabeled dataset
Measuring how stable cluster assignments are across multiple random restarts of K-Means
External ground-truth class labels are available to compare against the cluster assignments
explanation: adjusted_rand_score measures agreement between cluster labels and true labels, correcting for chance. Use silhouette/inertia when ground truth is unavailable.

q: Lasso (L1) regression is preferred over Ridge (L2) when the goal includes feature selection because:
options: Lasso drives the coefficients of irrelevant features exactly to zero, performing automatic selection
Lasso penalizes the sum of squared coefficients, shrinking all features proportionally but never reducing them exactly to zero
Lasso applies recursive feature elimination to iteratively remove the least important features
Lasso projects features into an orthogonal subspace using principal component decomposition
explanation: L1 penalty geometry produces sparse solutions: irrelevant feature coefficients become exactly 0. L2 (Ridge) shrinks all coefficients but rarely zeroes them.

q: Why must SMOTE be applied only to training data and never to the test set?
options: SMOTE requires access to class labels which are unavailable for the test set in production
The test set must reflect the real-world class distribution; synthetic samples would bias evaluation
SMOTE creates near-duplicate rows that would artificially inflate any metric on the test set
Applying SMOTE to the test set leaks training distribution statistics into evaluation
explanation: Evaluation must simulate real-world conditions; synthetically oversampling the test minority class would make the metric overly optimistic and misleading.

q: Why is np.log1p applied to the 'fare' column in Titanic feature engineering?
options: It standardizes the feature to zero mean, which accelerates gradient descent convergence
It converts the fare column from a continuous to an ordinal scale for tree-based models
It reduces the heavy right skew in fare's distribution, making it more symmetric for linear models
It maps fare values to the [0,1] range, preventing scale-sensitive algorithms from dominating
explanation: Fare has many cheap tickets and a few very expensive ones (right-skewed). log1p compresses the tail, making the distribution closer to normal for linear models.

q: Saving a full scikit-learn Pipeline (preprocessing + model) with joblib is recommended over saving just the model because:
options: Pipeline files are internally gzip-compressed, reducing artifact size by up to 10×
joblib cannot serialize individual estimator objects and requires them to be Pipeline-wrapped
Pipelines automatically embed SHA hashes for reproducible versioned artifact loading
It ensures preprocessing and model are always applied together, preventing serving-time mismatches
explanation: If the scaler is saved separately, a deployment might accidentally apply wrong statistics or skip scaling entirely. One Pipeline file eliminates that risk.

q: The /health endpoint in a FastAPI ML service is required primarily by:
options: Kubernetes liveness/readiness probes and load balancers checking service availability
MLflow experiment tracking, which polls health endpoints to record serving statistics
Prometheus, which uses the health endpoint to scrape prediction count metrics at fixed intervals
FastAPI's OpenAPI generator, which lists all registered routes including health in the schema
explanation: Kubernetes probes /health to decide whether to route traffic to a pod. Without it, crashed pods keep receiving requests silently.

q: Calling model.eval() before inference in PyTorch is necessary to:
options: Freeze all learnable parameters so they cannot be modified by subsequent backward passes
Disable training-specific behaviors like Dropout and BatchNorm that differ between train and eval
Convert the model to ONNX format for cross-platform deployment outside of Python
Move model weights to CPU memory because CUDA tensors cannot be used in evaluation mode
explanation: Dropout randomly zeros neurons and BatchNorm uses batch statistics during training. model.eval() switches both to deterministic inference behavior.

q: For a fraud detection dataset with 99.9% non-fraud and 0.1% fraud, which metric correctly evaluates a detector?
options: Accuracy, since it measures overall correctness across all classes in the dataset
Precision, since minimizing false alarms is the primary constraint in fraud alerting
F1-score or AUC-ROC, since accuracy misleads when class imbalance is this severe
R-squared, measuring what proportion of label variance the model explains
explanation: A model predicting 'no fraud' always gets 99.9% accuracy. F1 and AUC-ROC capture minority-class detection ability that accuracy hides.

q: Concept drift differs from data drift in that concept drift means:
options: Concept drift degrades only serving latency while data drift degrades prediction quality
Concept drift is caused by data pipeline bugs; data drift is a natural statistical phenomenon
Concept drift is detected via PSI on input features; data drift is detected via KS test on labels
The conditional relationship P(Y|X) changes so correct answers shift even for identical inputs
explanation: Data drift: P(X) shifts. Concept drift: P(Y|X) shifts—e.g., 'high income → churns more' becomes 'high income → churns less' after economic change.

q: In the two-sample KS test used for feature drift detection, a p-value below 0.05 means:
options: The two samples come from significantly different distributions, indicating likely feature drift
The two samples are statistically identical and no feature drift has occurred
The feature has low variance and is a poor predictor regardless of any distribution shift
The test lacked statistical power to detect a meaningful distributional difference
explanation: KS test null hypothesis is that both samples come from the same distribution. p < 0.05 rejects that null—the distributions differ significantly.

q: What is the correct end-to-end ML workflow sequence described in this chapter?
options: Preprocess → Load → EDA → Split → Scale → Train → Evaluate
Load → EDA → Preprocess → Split → Scale → Train → Evaluate
Load → Split → EDA → Preprocess → Train → Scale → Evaluate
EDA → Load → Split → Preprocess → Scale → Train → Evaluate
explanation: Standard workflow: Load data first, explore (EDA), preprocess, then split BEFORE scaling, then scale train set, train model, evaluate on held-out test set.

q: In Stratified K-Fold cross-validation, 'stratified' means that:
options: Folds are created in time order to simulate real-world temporal model generalization
The dataset is sorted by target label before dividing it into K equal folds
Each fold maintains the same class proportions as the full training dataset
Training folds are augmented with SMOTE before each fitting step in the loop
explanation: Stratification ensures each fold's class ratio mirrors the full dataset—critical for imbalanced data where a random fold might have no minority-class samples.

q: In an MLflow Model Registry, the 'Archived' stage means the model:
options: Its artifact file has been permanently deleted from the registry to free storage
The model is returned to Staging for re-evaluation and performance comparison against a newly trained challenger model
The model's predictions are cached so it can serve traffic without reloading weights
The model is retained for potential rollback but no longer actively serves production traffic
explanation: Archived models are preserved for audit, rollback, and comparison but are superseded by the current Production model. They are not deleted.

q: When class_weight='balanced' is set in a scikit-learn classifier, the algorithm adjusts:
options: The loss contribution of each sample inversely proportional to its class frequency in training data
The number of decision leaves so each class contributes an equal number of terminal nodes
The classification threshold from 0.5 to the minority class prevalence in the training set
The optimizer learning rate is reduced to slow down convergence specifically on the majority-class training examples
explanation: 'balanced' computes sample weights = n_samples / (n_classes * class_count) so minority-class samples contribute more to the loss function.

q: The recommended two-phase approach to hyperparameter tuning described in this chapter is to:
options: Always use Grid Search since it is guaranteed to find the globally optimal configuration
Start with Randomized Search to find promising regions, then refine with Grid Search
Use Randomized Search for discrete parameters only and Grid Search for continuous ranges
Run both methods in parallel and take the intersection of their top-ranked configurations
explanation: Randomized Search efficiently explores wide ranges; once promising hyperparameter regions are identified, Grid Search refines within that narrower space.

q: What is the key difference between Ridge and Lasso regularization regarding the resulting model?
options: Ridge drives some coefficients to exactly zero for feature selection; Lasso shrinks all proportionally
Ridge uses squared error as the prediction loss; Lasso uses absolute error as the prediction loss
Lasso can drive coefficients exactly to zero; Ridge only shrinks them toward zero without zeroing
Ridge requires mandatory cross-validation tuning while Lasso uses a fixed default alpha of 1.0
explanation: L1 (Lasso) geometry: the penalty constraint creates corners at the axes where optimal solutions land, producing exact zeros. L2 (Ridge) creates a smooth ball with no corners.

q: MLOps Maturity Level 2 includes which capability beyond Level 1?
options: Basic operational monitoring dashboards and calendar-based scheduled retraining jobs
Experiment tracking linked to a model registry with versioned artifact management
Human-in-the-loop review gates before any model is promoted from Staging to Production
CI/CD for model code, drift detection, canary deployments, and automated retraining triggers
explanation: Level 1 adds experiment tracking + registry + scheduled retraining. Level 2 adds CI/CD, drift detection, canary rollouts, and auto-triggered retraining pipelines.

q: In the FastAPI serve.py code, loading the pipeline at module startup rather than per-request is critical because:
options: Loading a pickle file per request adds 100–1000 ms overhead that violates typical latency SLAs
Pydantic validators require a global model reference for input schema inference at startup
FastAPI's dependency injection cannot inject per-request artifact objects efficiently
joblib.load acquires an exclusive file lock preventing concurrent model loads from multiple workers
explanation: Model deserialization from disk is expensive. Doing it inside the request handler would add seconds of latency; loading once at startup keeps per-request work negligible.

q: A low silhouette score for a data point indicates that the point:
options: Belongs to a cluster with too few members relative to other clusters in the partition
Is closer to points in a neighboring cluster than to other points within its own cluster
Is far from its centroid, which inflates the inertia metric without affecting silhouette
Is an outlier that DBSCAN would label as noise in a density-based clustering approach
explanation: Silhouette = (b − a) / max(a, b) where a = intra-cluster mean distance and b = nearest-cluster mean distance. Low values mean b ≈ a (point is near a border).

q: In the canary routing function shown in the chapter, user assignment to a model version is deterministic because:
options: Each user generates a session-scoped random number at login that persists for the session
Sticky sessions in the load balancer route users to the same backend pod consistently
A hash of the user_id is taken modulo 100 to assign a consistent percentage bucket
User IDs are sorted alphabetically and bucketed by first character to each model version
explanation: hashlib.md5(user_id) % 100 produces a stable bucket per user_id. Same user always gets the same model version, enabling accurate per-user business-metric analysis.

q: What does drop_first=True do when passed to pd.get_dummies()?
options: It drops the most frequent category to reduce class imbalance in the encoded columns
It drops the first row of the encoded DataFrame to eliminate redundant initialization artifacts from the encoding output
It sorts categories alphabetically before encoding and drops the first sorted entry
It removes one encoded column to avoid the dummy variable trap (perfect multicollinearity in linear models)
explanation: With K categories, K-1 binary columns fully encode the information. The K-th column is linearly predictable from the others, causing multicollinearity for linear models.

q: The PSI (Population Stability Index) is computed by:
options: Binning the reference and current distributions and summing a log-ratio formula over the bins
Computing pairwise Euclidean distances between reference and current feature vectors
Measuring the area under the ROC curve computed separately for reference and current windows
Running a chi-squared test comparing categorical frequency tables from two time periods
explanation: PSI = Σ (cur% − ref%) × ln(cur% / ref%) over N bins. It quantifies distributional shift using relative entropy between bucketed distributions.

q: Online serving differs from batch serving in that online serving:
options: Uses precomputed predictions stored in a feature store for sub-millisecond response time
Responds to individual requests in real time with a strict latency SLA (e.g., under 100 ms)
Processes large datasets in parallel across distributed nodes on a scheduled cadence
Stores raw model outputs in a database for later aggregation and downstream reporting
explanation: Online serving = request → model → response in milliseconds, serving one or a few rows. Batch serving scores large datasets on a schedule (nightly, weekly).

q: What does the n_informative parameter control in sklearn.datasets.make_classification?
options: The number of output classes in the generated synthetic classification dataset
The total number of features including noise and redundant features in the dataset
The number of features that genuinely contribute predictive signal for the target label
The minimum number of samples per class required for statistical significance
explanation: n_informative sets how many of the n_features features actually contain class signal. Remaining features are noise/redundant, making the problem more realistic.

q: Which tool in the MLOps ecosystem listed in the chapter is specifically designed for detecting distribution drift in production?
options: MLflow, which tracks training metrics and logs model artifacts across experiment runs
Weights & Biases (W&B), which provides dashboards and sweep visualization for hyperparameters
Kubeflow Pipelines, which orchestrates multi-step ML workflows on Kubernetes clusters
Evidently AI, which monitors feature distributions and prediction drift in deployed models
explanation: Evidently AI generates drift reports comparing training and production data. MLflow and W&B focus on experiment tracking, not production monitoring.

q: In K-Means, the 'inertia' metric measures:
options: The total sum of squared distances from each data point to its assigned cluster centroid
The ratio of inter-cluster variance to intra-cluster variance across all cluster assignments
The average silhouette score normalized to the [-1, 1] range across all data points
The fraction of total variance in the dataset explained by the current cluster solution
explanation: Inertia = Σ ||xi − centroid_j||² summed over all points i in their assigned cluster j. Lower inertia = tighter clusters but always decreases as K increases.

q: The 'naive' assumption in Naive Bayes is that:
options: The decision boundary between classes is a linear hyperplane in the feature space
All features are conditionally independent of each other given the class label
Prior class probabilities are equal for all classes regardless of training distribution
Feature distributions within each class are Gaussian (applied only by GaussianNB)
explanation: Naive Bayes assumes P(x1,x2,...,xn | y) = P(x1|y) × P(x2|y) × ... Despite this unrealistic assumption, it works well for text classification.

q: Which scikit-learn class applies separate preprocessing transformations to different column subsets in one step?
options: sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline, which chains transformations sequentially across all features
sklearn.preprocessing.FunctionTransformer, which applies a user-defined function to the entire feature matrix uniformly
sklearn.compose.ColumnTransformer, which dispatches numeric and categorical columns to separate pipelines
sklearn.impute.IterativeImputer, which models each feature using all other features jointly
explanation: ColumnTransformer takes a list of (name, transformer, columns) tuples and applies each transformer to its specified columns, then concatenates the outputs.

q: In the Titanic end-to-end project, the 'is_alone' feature is derived from:
options: The 'deck' column indicating whether the passenger occupied a private cabin
The 'who' column with values 'man', 'woman', or 'child' passenger type classification
The absence of a ticket number shared with other passengers in the same booking
The computed family_size column, where family_size == 1 means no accompanying relatives
explanation: family_size = sibsp + parch + 1; is_alone = (family_size == 1).astype(int). A passenger traveling without spouse, siblings, or children has family_size = 1.

q: When RandomizedSearchCV is set to n_iter=50 with cv=5, how many model fits are performed in total?
options: 50 model fits total, each evaluated once on a held-out validation fold
250 model fits total (50 hyperparameter combos × 5 cross-validation folds)
50 model fits per parameter, totaling 50 × number_of_parameters fits overall
5 model fits, each using a random 50-sample bootstrap from the training data
explanation: Each of the 50 randomly sampled hyperparameter configurations is evaluated via 5-fold CV, so 50 × 5 = 250 total fits.

q: When ground-truth cluster labels are not available, this chapter recommends evaluating K-Means by:
options: Using accuracy score between predicted cluster IDs and nearest known reference labels
Using both the silhouette score and the Elbow Method (inertia) together for reliability
Computing cross-entropy loss averaged over all cluster probability assignments
Running adjusted mutual information between cluster labels and the original feature matrix
explanation: Inertia alone always favors more clusters; silhouette score penalizes clusters that are not compact and well-separated. Using both gives a more reliable choice.

q: mlflow.models.infer_signature is called when logging a model to the registry in order to:
options: Extract model weights and convert them to ONNX format for cross-framework deployment
Automatically select the best-performing run and promote it to the Staging stage
Record the expected input feature schema and output schema for validation at serving time
Generate a confusion matrix artifact from the model's predictions on a validation set
explanation: The signature captures input column names/types and output shape. At serving time, the registry can validate that incoming requests match the trained model's expected schema.

q: Loading a PyTorch model with model.load_state_dict(torch.load('weights.pth')) requires that you:
options: Save the model using torch.save(model, ...) rather than torch.save(model.state_dict(), ...)
Call torch.cuda.empty_cache() first to free GPU memory before loading the checkpoint
Instantiate the same model architecture class first and then load the weights into it
Use model.eval() before loading because load_state_dict cannot run in training mode
explanation: state_dict contains only the weights (tensors), not the architecture. You must define IrisNet() (the class) first, then call model.load_state_dict(...) to populate it.

q: The ML CI/CD pipeline in the chapter is configured to exit non-zero and block the merge when:
options: The trained model's evaluation metric (e.g., F1) falls below a predefined quality threshold
The training data has more than 10% missing values in any numeric feature column
The model's hyperparameters differ significantly from the previous production run's best config
The experiment tracking server is unreachable or returns a network timeout error
explanation: train.py uses --validate-thresholds and exits with a non-zero code if metrics drop below acceptable bounds, blocking the CI pipeline from promoting the artifact.

q: The main risk of deploying a Kubernetes ML service without a /health liveness probe is:
options: The model will serve stale predictions because pod weights are never refreshed after load
The cluster continues routing traffic to a crashed or deadlocked pod, causing silent failures
Kubernetes schedules the pod on the highest-utilization node instead of an optimal one
The service will be invisible to the MLflow model registry as an active deployment endpoint
explanation: Without a liveness probe, Kubernetes has no signal that a pod is unhealthy. It keeps routing requests to a broken pod, silently degrading user experience.

q: SelectKBest with score_func=f_classif selects features based on:
options: Permutation importance computed by measuring accuracy drop when each feature is shuffled
Lasso regularization coefficients from a sparse linear model fit to the training data
ANOVA F-statistic measuring the linear dependency between each feature and the class target
Mutual information scores that capture both linear and non-linear feature-target relationships
explanation: f_classif computes the one-way ANOVA F-statistic for each feature vs. the target. Higher F means the feature better discriminates between classes.

q: P95 and P99 latency percentiles are monitored separately on a serving endpoint because:
options: Both must stay below the SLA threshold independently for the service to pass SRE compliance audits
P99 drives autoscaling policy while P95 triggers model-quality alerting in the monitoring pipeline
Together P95 and P99 define the normal operating band used in anomaly detection for traffic spikes
P99 exposes worst-case latency for the slowest 1% of requests that mean or median would conceal
explanation: Mean latency can look fine while 1% of users experience 5-second waits. P99 captures those tail experiences; P95/P99 together show the shape of the tail distribution.
